primary_diagnosis.name: Acute traumatic pretibial laceration with controllable venous bleeding and a situational (acute) panic reaction, following a mechanical trip in a person with congenital blindness whose home orientation was disrupted

comorbidities.name: Congenital blindness, both eyes (no light perception)

pathophysiology_summary: A person with lifelong congenital blindness navigates safely by an accurate internal map of a known environment plus tactile and auditory cues. A visiting relative rearranged the furniture, invalidating that map; combined with recent circadian disruption and fatigue, this produced a mechanical trip over a relocated chair. The trip caused a ~4 cm gaping laceration of the pretibial skin with active but venous (low-pressure, compressible) bleeding — no arterial spurt, no fracture, and intact distal neurovascular function. The sudden loss of environmental control plus pain and fright triggered an acute sympathetic (panic) response: tachycardia, tachypnoea, tremor and diaphoresis, with a modestly raised blood pressure — physiologically proportional to pain and fear rather than a primary cardiac or haemorrhagic event. The correct in-person management is wound irrigation, local-anaesthetic infiltration and primary closure with tetanus review; the telehealth-correct management is to recognise the need for urgent same-day professional closure, coach direct-pressure haemostasis, de-escalate the panic, and mitigate the recurrent-fall hazard.

differential.diagnosis: Traumatic pretibial laceration (soft-tissue injury from the fall)
differential.evidence_basis: Direct mechanical trauma over the shin with an obvious bleeding wound.
Pounding heart, fast breathing, tremor and terror that came on with the fright of the fall.
Any fall with lower-limb injury must screen for fracture before attributing pain to the wound alone.

differential.diagnosis: Syncope or pre-syncope as the cause of the fall
differential.evidence_basis: A collapse mislabelled as a trip can hide a cardiac/neurological cause; must be actively excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Both the injury and the panic are visible up front; the skill is to hold fracture and syncope open until the mechanism and red-flag screen exclude them, rather than anchoring on the obvious wound.

differential.diagnosis: Traumatic pretibial laceration with situational panic
differential.evidence_basis: Clear mechanical trip, controllable venous ooze, intact foot movement/sensation, weight-bearing possible with help, no chest pain — the injury plus a proportional panic response.
Made unlikely by brief weight-bearing with assistance, intact distal neurovascular function and no described deformity — but cannot be fully excluded via telehealth without examination ± imaging.
Clear tripping mechanism, no prodromal dizziness, no loss of consciousness — a mechanical, not a collapse, event.
No chest pain; the tachycardia and tachypnoea are proportional to acute pain and panic.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage the picture is a wound-plus-panic in a stable patient; retaining fracture as a residual telehealth uncertainty (cannot examine) is correct, while syncope and ACS are reasonably excluded on history.

differential.diagnosis: Traumatic pretibial laceration requiring urgent same-day primary closure, with a resolving situational panic reaction and a high recurrent-fall risk
differential.evidence_basis: Gaping dermis-deep wound with active (venous) bleeding needs professional closure today; panic settles with reassurance as the environment is restored; the disrupted layout is the modifiable fall hazard.

red_flags.description: Uncontrolled or arterial (spurting) haemorrhage from the laceration
red_flags.clinical_significance: Arterial or non-compressible bleeding is an immediate threat to life and would mandate 000; here the bleed is a venous ooze that slows with pressure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise escalating/uncontrolled bleeding could allow significant blood loss.

red_flags.description: Occult fracture or deep-structure (tendon/neurovascular) injury of the lower limb
red_flags.clinical_significance: Missed fracture or tendon/neurovascular injury leads to malunion or lasting disability; screened against by brief weight-bearing and intact distal movement/sensation, but not examinable via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed diagnosis of fracture or tendon injury.

red_flags.description: Syncope/collapse (cardiac or neurological) masquerading as a simple trip
red_flags.clinical_significance: A blackout cause of the fall would change the whole disposition; excluded here by a clear tripping mechanism with no prodrome and no loss of consciousness.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing an arrhythmia or other syncopal cause of falls.

red_flags.description: Acute coronary syndrome mislabelled as panic (chest pain with the tachycardia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Panic and ACS can look alike; chest pain is the discriminator and is absent here, with vitals proportional to pain/fear.
A head strike would require its own neuro observation pathway; the patient denies hitting her head.

red_flags.description: Wound infection developing over the following days (spreading redness, heat, pus, fever)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The at-risk period is after this encounter; a visually impaired patient cannot self-inspect the wound, so carer inspection and clear return advice are essential.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Cellulitis or wound sepsis progressing unnoticed.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the tachycardia, hypertension and distress entirely to 'anxiety because she is blind and panicking' and skipping the screen for fracture, significant blood loss, head injury or a cardiac/syncopal cause of the fall. The vitals here are proportional to pain and fear, but that must be reasoned to, not assumed from the disability.
Treating the injury as trivial or expected ('blind people fall') and under-investigating, or conversely catastrophising the whole presentation because of the disability.

Dispatching an ambulance / directing to ED for a compressible venous ooze in a stable patient — traumatic and resource-wasteful, and especially distressing transport for a disoriented blind patient — when urgent same-day in-person closure is the right level.
Reassuring the patient to simply bandage the wound at home, or attempting to coach self-closure, and missing that a gaping dermis-deep laceration needs professional closure today with tetanus review.

cannot_determine_via_chat: True wound depth and whether tendon, muscle or neurovascular structures are involved
Whether an underlying fracture is present (requires examination and possibly imaging)
Accurate blood pressure and heart rate (clinician-measured: BP 155/95, HR 110 in the source)
Fundoscopic and ocular examination findings (optic disc pallor, absent pupillary light reflex, roving eye movements) — clinician exam of the baseline blindness
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the laceration needs urgent same-day in-person professional closure (GP / urgent care); coach the spouse to maintain firm direct pressure and elevate the leg to control the venous bleeding; talk the patient down from the panic with slow breathing and grounding; confirm tetanus is current; arrange supported transport given her disorientation; and escalate to ED/000 only if bleeding becomes uncontrolled or she shows signs of significant blood loss.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the injury itself (an obvious wound after a witnessed trip); the real risks are mislabelling everything as 'just a panic attack' and under-screening, or over-reacting to a minor controllable bleed with an ambulance.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / standard wound-care guidance on traumatic laceration primary closure
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Gaping full-thickness lacerations benefit from early (same-day) irrigation and primary closure to reduce infection risk and improve healing; assess neurovascular and tendon function and tetanus status.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Australian Immunisation Handbook — tetanus prophylaxis in wound management
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Determine tetanus immunisation status for all wounds; no booster needed if fully up to date, as here.
